text: I have posted this video in hopes that it will be useful for residents and surgeons learning the transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair.
The inguinal anatomy is complicated. Admittedly, I found it tricky to fully understand it when performing open inguinal hernia repairs in residency.
The preperitoneal approach can be daunting at first, but I feel it is ultimately easier to understand from an internal perspective.
I have posted this video at full length and at real speed.
A few points are worth mentioning. I do not use an assist port for this operation.
However, one is present here because I later performed a parasophageal hernia repair after the inguinal hernia repair was complete.
The surgeon places five ports before docking the robot, using one of the available eight-millimeter ports to pass sutures and mesh.
The surgeon prepares to use a 10 by 15 centimeter ProGrip mesh, likely positioning it within the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal lining using an absorbable 2-0 V-Loc suture, followed by closing a small defect in the peritoneum with a 3-0 Vicryl suture on an S-H needle.

As long-winded as I am in other videos, I do not think I can talk for an entire twenty-two minutes, so I'll spare you the narration and simply entertain you with a few of my favorite songs. I hope you find this video useful.
